passages.text: The aesthetic sphere is the sphere of immediacy, the ethical the sphere of requirement (and this requirement is so infinite that the individual always goes bankrupt), the religious the sphere of fulfillment, but, please note, not a fulfillment such as when one fills an alms box or a sack with gold, for repentance has specifically created a boundless space, and as a consequence the religious contradiction: simultaneously to be out on 70,000 fathoms of water and yet be joyful.
passages.source: Stages on Life's Way, quoted in Either/Or commentary
passages.context: This is Kierkegaard's most compressed account of the three stages. The ethical stage is specifically characterized by infinite requirement that guarantees bankruptcy — the law cannot be fulfilled. The religious stage is not completion but a paradoxical condition: simultaneously drowning and joyful. This is not resolution but a higher-order tension that can only be sustained through faith, not reason.

passages.text: The aesthete, according to Kierkegaard's model, will eventually find himself in 'despair', a psychological state that results from a recognition of the limits of the aesthetic approach to life. The natural reaction is to make an eventual 'leap' to the second phase, the 'ethical,' which is characterized as a phase in which rational choice and commitment replace the capricious and inconsistent longings of the aesthetic mode.
passages.source: Either/Or, Volume I structure
passages.context: The movement from aesthetic to ethical is not gradual improvement but a discontinuous leap triggered by despair — the collapse of the aesthetic strategy of perpetual novelty and pleasure-seeking. The leap is existential, not logical: it cannot be argued for from within the aesthetic stance, only experienced as a crisis of that stance. Despair is thus productive: it is the condition of possibility for transformation.

passages.text: Anxiety is a qualification of dreaming spirit, and as such it has its place in psychology. Awake, the difference between myself and my other is posited; sleeping, it is suspended; dreaming, it is an intimated nothing. The actuality of the spirit constantly shows itself as a form that tempts its possibility but disappears as soon as it seeks to grasp for it, and it is a nothing that can only bring anxiety.
passages.source: The Concept of Anxiety, Nichol translation, p. 42
passages.context: Kierkegaard locates anxiety not in a threat but in the dreamlike encounter with pure possibility. Anxiety is 'freedom's actuality as the possibility of possibility' — not fear of a specific danger but the vertigo of having to be. The 'nothing' that produces anxiety is not emptiness but unlimited openness: the self must choose what it is from an infinite field with no pre-given guide.

passages.text: anxiety is not found in the beast, precisely because by nature the beast is not qualified as spirit.
passages.source: The Concept of Anxiety, Nichol translation, p. 42
passages.context: This brief observation carries substantial weight: anxiety is the mark of spirit, and spirit is what distinguishes the human from the animal. The animal acts from instinct without the burden of self-relation; the human must relate to itself, must choose, must be responsible — and this is the source of anxiety. Anxiety is therefore not a defect but the symptom of freedom, and freedom is what makes us human.

passages.text: Now he discovers that the self he chooses has a boundless multiplicity within itself inasmuch as it has a history, a history in which he acknowledges identity with himself. This history is of a different kind, for in this history he stands in relation to other individuals in the race, and to the whole race, and this history contains painful things, and yet he is the person he is only through this history. That is why it takes courage to choose oneself, for at the same time as he seems to be isolating himself most radically he is most radically sinking himself into the root by which he is bound up with the whole.
passages.source: Either/Or Part II, Hong translation, p. 216
passages.context: Kierkegaard's ethical stage turns on the act of choosing oneself — but this self is not a blank slate. It is a historically particular self with a painful history, embedded in a web of relations. The paradox Kierkegaard identifies is that the act of radical self-appropriation — which looks like isolation — is simultaneously the act of deepest rootedness. You cannot choose yourself without choosing your history, including its shame.

passages.text: In a spiritual sense that by which a person gives birth is the formative striving of the will and that is within a person's own power. What are you afraid of then? After all, you are not supposed to give birth to another human being; you are supposed to give birth only to yourself. And yet I am fully aware that there is an earnestness about this that shakes the entire soul; to become conscious in one's eternal validity is a moment that is more significant than everything else in the world.
passages.source: Either/Or Part II, Hong translation
passages.context: The image of giving birth to oneself is Kierkegaard's most concentrated figure for the ethical task: it requires labor, it is painful, and it produces something genuinely new — a self that has been freely appropriated rather than merely inherited. The phrase 'eternal validity' is key: by choosing oneself one does not merely make a temporary preference but establishes an identity that claims permanence.

passages.text: It is an earnest and significant moment when a person links himself to an eternal power for an eternity, when he accepts himself as the one whose remembrance time will never erase, when in an eternal and unerring sense he becomes conscious of himself as the person he is.
passages.source: Either/Or II, p. 206, Hong 1987, Judge Vilhelm
passages.context: Judge Vilhelm — the representative of the ethical stage — frames commitment not as limitation but as the achievement of eternal dimension. The self that commits enters time differently: it is no longer dispersed across possibilities but concentrated in a definite identity that time cannot dissolve. This is Kierkegaard's counter to the aesthetic flight from commitment: permanence as depth rather than prison.

passages.text: The self that is the objective is not only a personal self but a social, a civic self. He then possesses himself as a task in an activity whereby he engages in the affairs of life as this specific personality. Here his task is not to form himself but to act, and yet he forms himself at the same time, because, as I noted above, the ethical individual lives in such a way that he is continually transferring himself from one stage to another.
passages.source: Either/Or II, p. 262-263, Søren Kierkegaard
passages.context: The ethical self is both personal and civic — it cannot be achieved in isolation from concrete engagement with the world. Action and self-formation are not separate: the ethical person forms themselves through acting, not before acting. This refutes both pure inwardness (which never acts) and pure activism (which never reflects). The two are simultaneous in the ethical mode.

passages.text: You are outside yourself and therefore cannot do without the other as opposition; you believe that only a restless spirit is alive, and all who are experienced believe that only a quiet spirit is truly alive. For you a turbulent sea is a symbol of life; for me it is the quiet, deep water.
passages.source: Either/Or Part II, p. 144, Hong
passages.context: Judge Vilhelm's diagnosis of the aesthete: he requires external opposition and constant stimulus because he is not at home in himself. The contrast between the turbulent sea and the quiet deep water is Kierkegaard's image for the difference between the aesthetic mode (surface agitation, constant novelty) and the ethical mode (interior depth, stillness that is not emptiness but fullness).

passages.text: When a person turns and faces himself in order to understand himself, he steps, as it were, in the way of that first self, halts that which was turned outward in hankering for and seeking after the surrounding world that is its object, and summons it back from the external.
passages.source: Eighteen Upbuilding Discourses, 1845, Hong translation, p. 314
passages.context: Kierkegaard describes the foundational movement of self-knowledge as a literal turning: the self that has been outward-directed — grasping at objects, pleasures, stimuli — must turn back on itself. This is not narcissism but the condition of genuine selfhood. The deeper self 'lets the surrounding world remain dubious' as a way of making the outward-turned self release its grip on externals.

passages.text: There is only one way out, and that is to silence the deeper self by letting the roar of inconstancy drown it out.
passages.source: Eighteen Upbuilding Discourses, 1845, Hong translation, p. 314
passages.context: The alternative to self-confrontation is described with precise irony: the only escape from the deeper self's 'eternal claim' is to drown it in noise. This is the aesthetic strategy — perpetual stimulation, rotation, variety — understood as a form of flight. The aesthete is not enjoying life but fleeing from an interior demand. Kierkegaard names this flight without condemning it from outside; he lets its logic condemn itself.

passages.text: The tragic is that the two lovers don't understand each other; the comic is that two who do not understand each other love each other. That such a thing can happen is not inconceivable, for erotic love itself has its dialectic, and even if it were unprecedented, the construction, of course, has the absolute power to construct imaginatively.
passages.source: Stages on Life's Way, Hong, Letter to the Reader, p. 421
passages.context: Kierkegaard identifies the fundamental structure of romantic misunderstanding: the aesthetic lover understands love as feeling; the ethical lover understands it as commitment. Both are sincere, both love — yet they speak different languages. The observation that this is simultaneously tragic and comic is characteristic Kierkegaardian irony: the same situation viewed through different lenses yields opposite responses.

passages.text: In my opinion, all this beauty and cleverness, together with love and the eternal, have infinite worth, but without that a relation between man and woman, which nevertheless essentially wants to express this, is not worth a pipe of tobacco. In my opinion, when falling in love is separated from this—please note, the eternal from falling in love—one can properly speak only of what is left over.
passages.source: Stages on Life's Way, Hong, p. 292-293
passages.context: Kierkegaard argues that eros without the eternal is not love but its husk. The 'eternal' here is not religion specifically but the quality of infinite seriousness — the willingness to stake oneself absolutely on the relationship. Without this, even the most beautiful and intelligent connection 'revolves around what becomes nauseating because spirit qua spirit wants to have an ambiguous involvement with it.'

passages.text: There are three existence spheres: the esthetic, the ethical, the religious. To these there is receptively corresponding border territory: irony is the border territory between the esthetic and the ethical; humor is the confinium (border territory) between the ethical and the religious.
passages.source: Concluding Unscientific Postscript, p. 501-504
passages.context: Kierkegaard's mature account adds the transitional figures of irony and humor. Irony is what makes the aesthete begin to suspect the insufficiency of mere pleasure; it is the aesthetic mode reflexively undermining itself. Humor is what the ethical person uses to acknowledge that their infinite moral demand will never be fully met — it is the recognition of incommensurability that opens toward the religious.

passages.text: Irony is the unity of ethical passion, which in inwardness infinitely accentuates one's own I in relation to the ethical requirement—and culture, which in externality infinitely abstracts from the personal I as a finitude included among all other finitudes and particulars.
passages.source: Concluding Unscientific Postscript, p. 501-504
passages.context: Kierkegaard's definition of irony as a double movement — simultaneously infinite inward intensity and outward detachment — distinguishes it from mere sarcasm. The ironist is not indifferent; they are passionately committed inwardly while appearing cool outwardly. This double structure is what allows irony to be a genuine transition between stages rather than simply an aesthetic pose.

passages.text: Most people live in the opposite way. They are busy with being something when someone is watching them. If possible, they are something in their own eyes as soon as others are watching them, but inwardly, where the absolute requirement is watching them, they have no taste for accentuating the personal I.
passages.source: Concluding Unscientific Postscript, p. 501-504
passages.context: Kierkegaard's social critique: most people perform selfhood for an audience rather than constituting it inwardly. They are 'something' only when watched, which means their identity is borrowed from others' attention. The 'absolute requirement' — the ethical demand that comes from within — is precisely what they cannot bear to be alone with. This is the social version of the aesthetic flight from self-confrontation.

passages.text: There is a phantom that frequently prowls around when the making of a resolution is at stake—it is probability—a spineless fellow, as dabbler, a Jewish peddler, with whom no freeborn soul becomes involved, a good-for-nothing fellow who ought to be jailed instead of quacks, since he tricks people out of what is more valuable than money. Anyone who with regard to resolution comes no further, never comes any further than to decide on the basis of probability, is lost for ideality.
passages.source: Stages on Life's Way, Hong, p. 110
passages.context: Probability is Kierkegaard's name for the coward's substitute for commitment. The person who waits until the evidence is sufficient never commits — because certainty is never available. The genuine resolution must be made in the absence of guarantee, which is precisely what distinguishes it from mere calculation. To require probability before committing is to have already surrendered ideality to the market of outcomes.

passages.text: If a person does not encounter God in the resolution, if he has never made a resolution in which he had a transaction with God, he might just as well have never lived. But God always does business wholesale, and probability is a security that is not registered in heaven.
passages.source: Stages on Life's Way, Hong, p. 110
passages.context: Kierkegaard's religious intensification of the commitment problem: genuine resolution involves something absolute — God, or the unconditional — and any resolution made on probabilistic grounds is simply a preference, not a commitment. The merchant metaphor ('God does business wholesale') captures the all-or-nothing quality of genuine decision: partial commitment is no commitment at all.

passages.text: A Providence watches over each man's wandering through life. It provides him with two guides. The one calls him forward. The other calls him back. They are, however, not in opposition to each other, these two guides, nor do they leave the wanderer standing there in doubt, confused by the double call. Rather the two are in eternal understanding with each other. For the one beckons forward to the Good, the other calls man back from evil.
passages.source: Purity of Heart is to Will One Thing, Steere translation 1938, p. 39-40
passages.context: Kierkegaard offers a phenomenology of moral orientation: the forward call toward the good and the backward call of remorse are not contradictory but complementary guides. Remorse is rehabilitated here — it is not mere guilt but a productive guide that 'laboriously gathers up experience.' The person who only moves forward, without remorse, does not know the path as well as the one who has had to retrace their steps.

passages.text: The remorseful one, who comes behind, laboriously gathers up experience. The eager traveler hurries forward to the new, to the novel, and, indeed, away from experience. But the remorseful one, who comes behind, laboriously gathers up experience.
passages.source: Purity of Heart is to Will One Thing, Steere translation 1938, p. 39-40
passages.context: The contrast with the aesthetic mode is implicit but precise: the aesthete ('eager traveler') races forward toward the new and away from experience, while remorse is the act of returning to what was left behind. Kierkegaard here positions the ethical mode not as forward progress but as the willingness to go back and face what was evaded. The depth of experience belongs to those who have had to retrace their steps.

passages.text: Just as a man changes his clothes for celebration, so a person preparing for the holy act of confession is inwardly changed. It is indeed like changing one's clothes to divest oneself of multiplicity in order to make up one's mind about one thing, to interrupt the pace of busy activity in order to put on the repose of contemplation in unity with oneself. And this unity with oneself is the celebration's simple festive dress that is the condition of admittance.
passages.source: Upbuilding Discourses in Various Spirits, Søren Kierkegaard, 1847, Hong, p. 19
passages.context: Kierkegaard uses the image of changing clothes to describe the interior movement required for genuine self-examination. To 'divest oneself of multiplicity' is to stop being dispersed across competing desires and appearances, to achieve momentary unity. This unity — not a final state but a condition of receptivity — is what Kierkegaard means by purity of heart. It is achieved not by addition but by subtraction.

passages.text: Ask yourself and keep on asking until you find the answer, for one may have known something many times, acknowledged it; one may have willed something many times, attempted it—and yet, only the deep inner motion, only the heart's indescribable emotion, only that will convince you that what you have acknowledged belongs to you, that no power can take it from you—for only the truth that builds up is truth for you.
passages.source: Two Upbuilding Discourses, 1843, p. 9-12
passages.context: This is Kierkegaard's account of subjective appropriation — the difference between knowing something and that knowledge being truly yours. One can know and acknowledge repeatedly without genuine possession; the criterion for real possession is the 'heart's indescribable emotion,' which confirms that the truth has entered the self not as information but as transformation. This is what distinguishes Kierkegaardian 'subjective truth' from both opinion and objective fact.

passages.text: One must be either good or bad. You must cultivate either your own reason or else externals; apply yourself either to things within or without you—that is, be either a philosopher or one of the mob.
passages.source: Either/Or, Epictetus epigraph via Either/Or
passages.context: Kierkegaard opens Either/Or with this Epictetan challenge as a motto — the insistence that life demands a fundamental choice of orientation: inward or outward, reason or appearance. The 'either/or' of the title is not merely a logical connective but an existential demand. One cannot be partly a philosopher and partly 'one of the mob' without being authentically neither — the middle position is itself a kind of dishonesty.

passages.text: The esthetic sphere is the sphere of immediacy... Just as the ethical sphere is a passageway—which one nevertheless does not pass through once and for all—just as repentance is its expression, so repentance is the most dialectical. No wonder, then, that one fears it, for if one gives it a finger it takes the whole hand.
passages.source: Stages on Life's Way, Hong translation, p. 476-477
passages.context: The ethical sphere is a 'passageway' not a destination — one does not graduate through it but must continually inhabit it through the ongoing act of repentance. The observation that repentance 'takes the whole hand' captures its totalizing logic: once genuinely begun, repentance is infinite in its demands, since it moves backward through all prior choices. This is why it is feared: genuine repentance has no convenient stopping point.

passages.text: In connection with the truth as inwardness in existence, in connection with a more incorruptible joy of life, which has nothing in common with the craving of the life-weary for diversion, the opposite holds true; the law is: the same and yet changed, and still the same.
passages.source: Concluding Unscientific Postscript, p. 254-255, Swenson and Lowrie translation
passages.context: This formulation captures Kierkegaard's alternative to the aesthetic 'rotation method' — the strategy of perpetual novelty to ward off boredom. The law of inward existence is not variety but 'the same and yet changed': genuine repetition, which is not mere sameness but the continual deepening of what one has already chosen. This is what committed love, committed faith, and committed selfhood all share — they are repetitions that go deeper rather than rotations that seek the new.
